title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAX APPEAL NUMBER E1415 OF 2024
BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED………………….………...…...
APPELLANT
-VERSUS-
COMMISSIONER OF INVESTIGATIONS &
ENFORCEMENT...........RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a registered tax payer whose principal business
activity is in the construction industry.
2. The Respondent is a principal officer appointed under Section 13
of the Kenya Revenue Authority Act, CAP 469 of Kenya’s Laws
(hereinafter “the Act”). Under Section 5 (1) of the Act, the Kenya
Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government for the
collection and receipt of all tax revenue. Further, under Section
5(2) of the Act with respect to the performance of its functions
under subsection (1), the Authority is mandated to administer and
enforce all provisions of the written laws as set out in Part 1 and 2
of the First Schedule to the Act for the purposes of assessing,
collecting and accounting for all revenues in accordance with
those laws.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 1 of 30

3. The Appellant was issued with Income Tax assessments in relation
to the years 2020 and 2021 and VAT assessments for the periods
2019, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020,
September 2020 and December 2020 totalling to Kshs.
17,035,863.04.
4. The Appellant lodged an objection to the said assessments on 4th
October 2024.The Respondent issued its objection decision dated
7th November 2024, fully rejecting the Appellant's objection.
5. Aggrieved by the Respondent's decision of 7th November 2024, the
Appellant lodged a Notice of Appeal dated 26th May, 2025 and filed
on 30th May 2025.
THE APPEAL
6. The Appellant lodged its memorandum of appeal dated 5th
December, 2024 and filed on 6th December, 2024 wherein it raised
the following grounds of appeal:
(i) That the Respondent erred in law and fact by overestimating
the Appellant's turnover and disregarding operational
expenses incurred by the Appellant when raising the alleged
Income Tax assessments.
(ii) That the Respondent erred in law and fact by rejecting the
input VAT claimed by the company in generating its additional
assessments which contravenes Section 5(3) and Section
17(1) of the Value Added Tax Act, CAP 476 of the Laws of
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 2 of 30

Kenya (hereinafter “VATA”) and violates the right to deduct
input VAT as enshrined in the VATA.
(iii) That the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to
consider that the taxpayers from whom the vatable supplies
were obtained were and still are registered' for VAT purposes
and the company having carried out its due diligence to
confirm the same, including querying their status from the
KRA itax portal did not have any reason to disbelief their
status.
(iv) Contravening Section 4(3)(g) of the Fair Administrative Actions
Act, CAP 7L of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “FAAA”) which
mandates administrative bodies to give persons affected by a
decision information, materials and evidence to be relied upon
in making the decision or taking the administrative action.
APPELLANT’S CASE
7. In support of the appeal, the Appellant lodged statement of facts
dated 5th December, 2024 and filed on 6th December, 2024.
8. The Appellant averred that the Respondent erred in law and fact
by misunderstanding the operations of the Appellant and
proceeding to disallow input VAT claimed by the Appellant on
invoices purportedly not declared by a particular supplier. That the
Respondent therefore erred in law and fact by failing to consider
that the claimed input VAT were solely for the purpose of making
taxable supplies.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 3 of 30

9. The Appellant averred that it is engaged in the business of
construction of infrastructural projects and in the course of
conducting such projects it deals with different suppliers for
Vatable inputs required as raw materials for construction.
10. The Appellant asserted that the claimed input taxes were
legitimate claims in accordance with the following provisions of
Section 17(1) of the VATA which allows deduction of input VAT:
"Subject to the provisions of this section and the regulations,
input tax on a taxable supply to, or importation made by, a
registered person may, at the end of the tax period in which the
supply or importation occurred, be deducted by the registered
person, subject to the exceptions provided under this section,
from the tax payable by the person on supplies by him in that tax
period, but only to the extent that the supply or importation was
acquired to make taxable supplies."
11. The Appellant stated that in the case of Highlands Mineral
Water Limited v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2021]
eKLR, the High Court confirmed this position by stating as follows:
"The right to deduction of input tax is an integral part of the VAT
scheme as a taxable person who makes a transaction in respect
of which VAT deductible may deduct the VAT in respect of goods
and services acquired by him provided that such goods and
services have a direct and immediate link with the output
transaction in respect of which VAT is deductible"
12. The Appellant also averred that this position was reiterated in the
case of Shreeji Enterprises (K) Ltd v Commissioner of
Investigations and Enforcement 58 & 186 of 2019 where the
Tribunal held as follows:
"a taxpayer is legally entitled to claim input VAT where is has
properly discharged its duty of paying output Value Added
Tax (VA]) and supplying corresponding information to the
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 4 of 30

effect that it had purchased from a registered persons prior
to claiming input tax".
13. The Appellant averred that the claimed inputs were taxable
supplies and therefore allowable for tax purposes. That it was
therefore erroneous and unlawful for the Respondent to disallow
and prevent the Appellant from exercising its rights.
14. That the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to consider
that the taxpayers from whom the vatable supplies were obtained
were and still are registered for VAT purposes and the company
having carried out its due diligence to confirm the same, including
querying their status from the KRA iTax portal did not have any
reason to disbelief their status.
15. The Appellant contends that Respondent failed to consider the
provisions of Section 17 of the VATA in raising additional
assessments by disallowing the Appellant's input VAT.
16. The Appellant posited that the basis of claiming input VAT are as
provided for and listed under Section 17 of the VATA in compliance
with the following additional requirements:
(a) The taxpayer is registered for VAT;
(b) The purchase was for purposes of making a taxable supply;
(c) The input tax does not relate to the excluded purchases as set
out in Section 17 (4) of the VATA or exempt supplies; and
(d) The input tax is claimed within 6 months of receiving the
supply.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 5 of 30

17. It was the Appellant's position that it complied with all the
requirements as set out in Section 17 of the VATA in respect of its
input VAT claims.
18. It was the Appellant's further position that declaration of the
purchases by the supplier under Section 17 (2) (b) of the VATA is
an alternative to possession of the requisite documents, and that
this would not apply where the Appellant holds documentation to
support claimed input VAT and that as indicated in the
Respondents Objection decision, the Appellant supplied some
documents on 24th October 2024 in support of its objection, these
formed the documents which the Appellant had control over in
addition to the invoices supplied earlier subject of the disallowed
input claims.
19. The Appellant stated that it had no control over how its suppliers
declare the sales in their VAT returns and it is prejudicial for the
Respondent to attribute non-compliance of the listed suppliers to
it. That failure by the supplies to declare sales cannot be the basis
of disallowing input VAT as the Appellant accounted for all output
VAT pursuant to the provisions of the VATA and there is no dispute
that there were vat-able supplies made by the Appellant to its
customers.
20. The Appellant averred that it was entitled to a legitimate
expectation that the Respondent would allow its input VAT claims
since at the time of claiming the input it held the required
documents and or the registered suppliers had declared the sales
invoices in their returns.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 6 of 30

21. That having deliberately ignored the invoices submitted by the
Appellant for review, the Respondent ended up raising the
impugned assessments subject of this dispute to the detriment of
the Appellant.
22. The Appellant averred that Article 47(1) of the Constitution of
Kenya, 2010 (hereinafter “the Constitution”) and Section 4 of the
FAAA accords every person the right to administrative action that
is expeditious, efficient, lawful, reasonable ad procedurally fair and
that if a right or fundamental freedom of a person has been or is
likely to be adversely affected by the administrative action, the
person has the right to be given written reasons for the action.
23. The Appellant contended that the Respondent's action of
disallowing input VAT which the Appellant is lawfully entitled to
claim amounts to is unfair and unlawful administrative action.
24. The Appellant stated that the claims by the Respondent in its
Objection decision that the supplier in question to the disallowed
invoices stated that 'it was unaware of the invoices the company
had claimed from it and had never received any payments from
Buffloc Investments Limited' remains hearsay and
unsubstantiated.
25. The Appellant averred that it indeed claimed legitimate purchases
and that the Respondent's decision to condemn its purchase
invoices as being non-existent is not only prejudicial but
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 7 of 30

subjective. That the Respondent's conclusion was based on
assumptions, lack of factual basis and such must fail.
26. The Appellant stated that had the Respondent appreciated all
explanations, documents and information provided by the
Appellant it would not have made the impugned decision.
Appellant’s Prayers
27. The Appellant prayed as follows:
(a) This Appeal be allowed;
(b) The Respondent's decision dated 7th November 2024 be set
aside and reversed;
(c) The costs of and incidental to this appeal be awarded to the
Appellant; and
(d) Any other orders the Tribunal may deem fit.
RESPONDENT’S CASE
28. The Respondent’s case was premised on its Statement of facts
dated and filed on 19th December, 2024.
29. The Respondent averred that it is not bound by the returns filed by
the Appellant and may, using the information at its disposal,
assess taxes. That this includes disallowing fictitious purchases or
input claims as the case herein.
30. The Respondent averred that the returns that had been submitted
by the Appellant pursuant to the provisions of Section 24(1) of the
Tax Procedures Act, CAP 469B of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter
“TPA”) were put to question following the receipt of intelligence
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 8 of 30

that the Appellant was involved in a missing trader scheme and as
such, was a beneficiary.
31. That the investigations carried out revealed that indeed the
Appellant was engaged in a missing trader scheme and therefore
the Respondent mandated by law under Section 24(2) of the TPA
proceeded to raise assessments by disallowing the fictitious inputs
claimed from the missing traders.
32. That from the above, the crux of the audit therefore, was whether
the Appellant implemented a transaction to avoid tax in the nature
of a missing trader scheme as provided for under the provisions of
Section 23 of the Income Tax Act, CAP. 470 Laws of Kenya
(hereinafter “ITA”).
33. The Respondent asserted that the case herein is about whether
transactions amounting to a missing trader scheme were
implemented by the Appellant and aimed at reducing its tax
liability and whether the Appellant successfully rebutted the
allegations placed upon it in discharge of its primary burden of
proof.
34. The Respondent posited that the Appellant failed to provide any
documentation in support of its contention other than the audited
financial accounts. That the Respondent clearly and unequivocally
guided that the documentation required were invoices, contracts,
receipts and evidence of payment.
35. The Respondent stated that the Appellant failed to rebut the
allegations by providing positive evidence in support, that it fell
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 9 of 30

short of discharging its primary burden of proof as provided for
under Section 56 of the TPA and Section 30 of the Tax Appeals
Tribunal Act, CAP 469A of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “TATA”).
36. The Respondent relied on the pronunciation by the Tribunal in TAT
Appeal No. E267 of 2023 - China Communications
Construction Company Limited v Commissioner,
Intelligence, Strategic Operations, Investigations and
Enforcement Department which quoted with approval the
following holding in TAT Appeal No. 1296 of 2022 - James
Finlay {Kenya) Limited v Commissioner of Domestic Taxes:
"From the above decision of the superior court, it is apparent
that once the Appellant is served with an assessment, the
Appellant ought to push back and show that the assessment
was incorrect by providing positive evidence."
37. That the positive evidence alluded by the Respondent refers to the
proof that there was an actual underlying commercial transaction
and that the documents provided i.e. invoices, delivery notes and
bank transfers were not a mere facade to veil the real mischief
being to evade tax or lower the tax burden through the missing
trader scheme.
38. The Respondent averred that the above position and the duty to
produce or tender positive evidence in support of its allegation
beyond the questioned documents was epitomised by the High
Court in the case of Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v
Galaxy Tools Limited (2021) eKLR where the Court stated as
follows:
''With greatest respect, the Tribunal got it wrong. What the
Respondent had done in producing the invoices, the delivery
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 10 of 30

notes and payment schedules was only prima facie evidence
of purchase. On producing the said documents, the
evidentiary burden of proof shifted to the appellant. The
appellant in answer not only queried the said documents but
informed the Tribunal that; he had carried investigations on
the alleged suppliers and concluded that they never existed,
that there was no supply of any goods at all. That the
documents produced did not contain critical details to support
any reasonable commercial transaction. All this was laid bare
before the Tribunal.
On the foregoing, the evidentiary burden of proof shifted back
to the respondent to show that its documentation was
legitimate. This would have been by production of other
transactional documentation to support the legitimacy of the
alleged transactions. It is at that juncture that sections 59 of
the Tax Procedures Act and section 43 of the VAT Act kicks
in."
39. The Respondent stated that at present, before the TAT, the
Appellant has not provided any documentation to rebut the
assertion by the Respondent that the Appellant employed a
missing trader scheme as it was the ultimate beneficiary and
therefore the taxes assessed were correct and enjoys a
presumption of correctness where the Appellant has not rebutted
the same and that since the goods in question alleged to have
been supplied are vatable, the disallowing of the fictitious
purchases automatically attracts VAT assessments which were
accordingly computed.
40. The Respondent averred that as held by the Tribunal in TAT
Appeal No. E267 of 2023 China Communications
Construction Company Limited v Commissioner,
Intelligence, Strategic Operations, Investigations and
Enforcement Department where it is established that the
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 11 of 30

taxpayer was involved in a tax evasion scheme, Section 23(3) of
the ITA justifies and upholds any assessments or adjustments
made by the Respondent regarding a transaction carried out to
avoid tax liability.
41. The Respondent asserted that in determining the issues raised by
the Appellant in relation to the application of Section 17 of the
VATA, the Commissioner was guided by the relevant provisions of
the VATA that is Section 17 (1) and (2) of the VATA which provide
as follows:
“ 1) Subject to the provisions of this Act and the regulations, input
tax on a taxable supply to, or importation made by, a registered
person may, at the end of the tax period in which the supply or
importation occurred, be deducted by the registered person,
subject to the exceptions provided under this section, from the tax
payable by the person on supplies by him in that tax period, but
only to the extent that the supply or importation was acquired to
make taxable supplies.
2) If, at the time when a deduction for input tax would
otherwise be allowable under subsection (1), (a) the person does
not hold the documentation referred to in subsection (3), or (b) the
registered supplier has not declared the sales invoice in a return,
the deduction for input tax shall not be allowed until the first tax
period in which the person holds such documentation. Provided
that the input tax shall be allowable for a deduction within six
months after the end of the tax period in which the supply or
importation occurred.”
42. The Respondent posited that having established that the
purchases had a direct and immediate link to sales in respect of
which input VAT was deductible the Commissioner analysed the
taxpayer's compliance with the requirements set out in Section 17
of the VAT Act for the deductibility of input VAT as follows: -
(a) Registration for VAT;
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 12 of 30

(b) Making taxable supplies and charging VAT thereof;
Input VAT claimed within the stipulated timelines; and
(c) Possession of the documents set out in Section 17(3)
of the VATA.
43. The Respondent stated that the first three requirements prima
facie are not in contention in this case and the Commissioner is
required to confirm whether the Appellant had the required
documents in support of the claim for input VAT.
44. That documents that must be held by a person to qualify for the
deduction of input VAT are detailed in Section 17(3) of the VATA as
follows:
(a) Original tax invoice issued for the supply or a certified
copy;
(b) Customs entry duly certified by the proper officer and
a receipt for the payment of tax;
(c) Customs receipt and a certificate signed by the proper
officer stating the amount of tax paid, in the case of goods
purchased from a customs auction;
(d) Credit note in the case of input tax deducted under
Section 16(2); or Debit note in the case of input tax
deducted under Section 16(5).
45. The Respondent averred further that in addition to the documents
listed above, the courts have stated in various cases that the right
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 13 of 30

to claim input VAT is hinged upon the proof of an underlying
commercial transaction, the Appellant failed to surmount all the
above issues and failed to discharge its burden of proof. The
Respondent relied on HCCOMM ITA No. Eo82 of 2021 Heet
Enterprises Limited v Commissioner Investigations and
Enforcement; and HCCOMM ITA No. 093 of 2022 - Kenya
Revenue Authority v Saj Ceramics Limited
46. That according to Section 17(1) of the VATA input tax is deductible
where it is incurred on purchases that it made. That this means
that for a taxpayer to deduct input tax, it must have actually made
a purchase and not in mere possession of the above documents
which in this case, the Appellant failed to provide despite several
reminders.
47. The Respondent asserted that the finding that the Appellant did
not provide any other supporting documentation other than the
audited financial accounts and findings from the investigations of
the existence of an elaborate missing trader scheme led to the
logical conclusion that the Appellant was the beneficiary of the
same.
48. That this corroborated the finding that the purchases were
fictitious and that the Appellant was a beneficiary of the missing
trader scheme and therefore the Respondent did not err in finding
that input claims by the Appellant failed to meet the requirements
of Section 17 of the Value Added Tax Act, 2013 as elaborated
above. That the above finding is supported by the binding decision
of the High Court in the case of HCCOMM ITA No. 093 of 2022 -
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 14 of 30

Commissioner Domestic Taxes v Sai Ceramics Limited where
the court surmised as follows:
“The Appellant (Authority) has a right to satisfy itself the
documents presented to it by the taxpayer are capable of
discharging the burden imposed on it. In the instant case
once, the Appellant rejected the Respondent's documents, the
burden shifted back to the Respondent, as the taxpayer, to
prove that the said documents are genuine.”
49. The Respondent argued that by the above responses it is clear
that the Appellant herein was granted an opportunity to present a
late objection and documents in support, the Appellant was also
given the basis and the reason for the assessments being fictitious
purchases by dint of being a beneficiary of a missing trader
scheme. In that regard, the Appellant has not demonstrated the
kind of documentation, information or evidence that it is alleged
not to have been supplied and that the Appellant’s contentions
that the Respondent contravened the provisions of Article 47 of
the Constitution and Section 4(3)(g) of the FAAA for failing to it the
information, materials and evidence relied on in making the
administrative decision is unfounded.
50. The Respondent averred that having notified the taxpayer on the
basis of assessments as has been outlined herein which was clear
and in unambiguous terms, the burden of proof shifted to the
taxpayer to disprove the Respondent. That the Appellant failed to
discharge this burden and has not demonstrated that it made any
inquiry or request for any information or record that was not
provided.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 15 of 30

51. The Respondent propounded that the taxpayer's contention on the
right to fair administrative action is also tampered with the fact
that it is settled law that persons exercising quasi-judicial powers
are masters of their own procedure as outlined in Kenya
Revenue Authority v Menginya Salim Murgani Civil Appeal
No. 108 of 2009, where the Court of appeal delivered itself as
follows: -
"There is ample authority that decision making bodies other than
courts and bodies whose procedures are laid down by statute are
masters of their own procedures. Provided that they achieve the
degree of fairness appropriate to their task it is for them to
decide how they will proceed."
52. That in this particular case, the Respondent gave the taxpayer an
opportunity to object to its tax assessments, heard the taxpayer,
reviewed the documentation and explanations required and
thereafter gave the reasoned basis for the rejection of the
objection and the nature of support that was not provided. That it
is settled law that the Appellant was required to provide positive
evidence to demonstrate that the purchases was valid and
genuine and in effect disprove the Commissioner, once this was
not done, the Appellant cannot be heard to blame the Respondent.
53. The Respondent asserted that the Appellant failed to plead with
clarity on how its rights to fair hearing and administrative action
act was infringed as provided for in the authority of Anarita
Karimi Njeru v Republic (Miscellaneous Criminal Application
4 of 1979) [1979] KEHC 30 (KLR) (Crim) (29 January 1979)
(Judgment).
Respondent’s Prayers
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 16 of 30

54. The Respondent sought the following reliefs:
(a) That the outstanding taxes plus penalties and interests
demanded are due payable; and
(b) That the Appellant's Appeal be hereby dismissed with costs to
the Respondent.
PARTIES’ WRITTEN SUBMISSIONS
Appellant’s Submissions
55. On 20th November, 2025 the Tribunal directed that parties file
written submissions.
Appellants Written Submissions
56. The Appellant filed its written submissions on 24th November, 2025
wherein it identified the issues for determination in this appeal to
be:
(i) Whether the Respondent erred in law and fact by rejecting the
Appellant’s input VAT contrary to Section 5(3) and 17(1) of the
VAT Act, 2013.
(ii) Whether the Respondent erred in disallowing the Appellant’s
input VAT despite the suppliers being duly registered and the
Appellant having exercised due diligence under the VAT Act.
(iii) Whether the Respondent’s decision contravened Article 47 of the
Constitution and Section 4(3)(g) of the Fair Administrative Action
Act, 2015.
57. The Appellant submitted that all suppliers from whom the vatable
supplies were obtained were duly registered for VAT. That prior to
transacting it verified each supplier’s registration status on the
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 17 of 30

Respondent’s i-Tax portal, thereby fulfilling its duty of due
diligence.
58. The Appellant submitted that Section 17(2) and 17(4) of the VATA
list conditions for input tax deduction: the taxpayer must be
registered, possess valid invoices, use the goods for taxable
supplies, and not claim tax on excluded or exempt items. That the
Appellant met all these statutory thresholds.
59. That the law does not impose an obligation on a purchaser to
ensure that the supplier remits output VAT. That once a purchaser
acts in good faith and holds valid documentation, the responsibility
for declaration lies squarely with the supplier, the Respondent’s
attribution of supplier non-compliance to the Appellant is therefore
misplaced and contrary to established principles of tax law.
60. The Appellant relied on Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v
Trical and Hard Limited at paragraph 26, where the High Court
affirmed that once a taxpayer produces competent and relevant
evidence, such as valid invoices and supporting documentation,
the evidential burden shifts to the Commissioner. That the Court
stated that competent documentation “demolishes the
presumption of correctness and swings the burden to the
Commissioner,” and that it would be unjust to hold a taxpayer
liable where the Commissioner has not produced evidence to
disprove what has been provided.
61. The Appellant submitted that in the present Appeal, it tendered all
required documents, and the Respondent failed to rebut them with
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 18 of 30

any contrary evidence, making the disallowance of input VAT
unlawful and unsubstantiated.
62. The Appellant submitted that furthermore, the doctrine of
legitimate expectation applies. That having complied with all
statutory provisions and acted transparently, the Appellant
legitimately expected that its properly supported claims would be
allowed, denying such claims violates the settled principle that
taxpayers are entitled to predictability and fairness in the
administration of tax laws.
63. That the Respondent’s reliance on alleged supplier omissions,
without producing the investigation reports or verifying the
invoices, breached the requirement of objective and evidence-
based assessment. That Section 29(1) of the TPA obliges the
Commissioner to base assessments on available information and
documents provided by the taxpayer.
64. The Appellant submitted that it had no control over the suppliers’
filing behaviour, that penalizing it for another taxpayer’s error
amounts to collective punishment, which is inconsistent with the
principles of equity and fairness enshrined in Article 47 of the
Constitution. That therefore, the Respondent’s actions were
unreasonable, contrary to the VATA, and offended the Appellant’s
right to fair administrative action.
65. The Appellant submitted that Article 47(1) of the Constitution
guarantees every person the right to administrative action that is
expeditious, efficient, lawful, reasonable, and procedurally fair.
That the Respondent, being a public body, is bound by this
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 19 of 30

constitutional command. Section 4(3)(g) of the FAAA further
provides as follows:
“Where an administrative action is likely to adversely affect the
rights or fundamental freedoms of any person, the administrator
shall give the person affected by the decision information,
materials and evidence to be relied upon in making the decision
or taking the administrative action.”
66. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent failed to furnish it
with the alleged supplier investigations, correspondence, or
statements relied upon in disallowing input VAT. That this omission
deprived the Appellant of a meaningful opportunity to respond and
thus violated procedural fairness. The Appellant relied on the case
of Republic v Kenya Revenue Authority Ex Parte Jaffer
Mohamed [2015] eKLR, where the High Court held as follows:
“A taxing authority is not entitled to pluck a figure from the air
and impose it upon a taxpayer without some rational basis for
arriving at that figure… Such action would be arbitrary,
capricious and in bad faith.”
67. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent’s objection decision
was premised on speculation and hearsay that a supplier was
unaware of certain invoices, without substantiating those
allegations.
68. That Respondent’s failure to engage constructively with the
Appellant, and its refusal to furnish written reasons for
disregarding the documentary evidence availed during the
objection process, amounted to a breach of Section 6(4) of the
FAAA. The law is explicit that where adverse administrative action
is taken, the taxpayer must be provided with written reasons. The
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 20 of 30

Respondent’s silence and unexplained rejection of evidence
rendered the objection decision arbitrary, irrational, and
procedurally unfair.
69. The Appellant submitted that while it acknowledges the statutory
obligation to comply with tax laws, it is equally the duty of the
Respondent to act within the confines of the law and to exercise
its mandate fairly and transparently. The Appellant relied on the
case of Republic v Kenya Revenue Authority Ex parte Bata
Shoe Company (Kenya) Ltd [2014] eKLR, where the Court
observed that:
“Payment of tax is an obligation imposed by the law. It is not a
voluntary activity. That being the case, a taxpayer is not
obliged to pay a single coin more than is due to the taxman.
The taxman on the other hand is entitled to collect up to the
last coin that is due...”
70. The Appellant submitted that this holding underscores the
principle that the Respondent must operate strictly within the
boundaries set by tax statutes and must not act arbitrarily.
Respondent’s written submissions
71. The Respondent filed written submissions dated and filed 21st
November, 2025 wherein it submitted that the issues for
determination in this matter are:
(i) Whether the Objection Decision is proper in law.
(ii) Whether the Appellant discharged its statutory burden of
proof under Section 56 TPA and Section 30 TAT Act .
(iii) Whether the Respondent erred in disallowing input VAT and
costs arising from fictitious purchases.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 21 of 30

(iv) Whether the Respondent violated the Appellant's right to
fair administrative action under Article 47 of the Constitution.
72. The Respondents submissions were a rehash of its statement of
facts, the Tribunal will therefore not reiterate the same.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
73. Having carefully evaluated the parties’ pleadings, submissions and
documentation, the Tribunal was of the respectful view that the
issues that call for its determination are as follows:
(a) Whether the Appellant discharged its statutory burden of
proof under Section 56 of TPA and Section 30 of TATA.
(b) Whether the Respondent erred in disallowing the
Appellant’s VAT input claims.
ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS
74. Having identified the issues that call for its determination the
Tribunal analysed the same as hereunder.
(a) Whether the Appellant discharged its statutory burden of
proof under Section 56 of TPA and Section 30 of TATA.
75. The Appellant’s case was that the Respondent erred by disallowing
input tax deductions which were legitimately incurred in the
course of the Appellant's business operations for the tax period
under review. It also stated that the input expenses in question
were directly related to the taxable supplies made by the
Appellant and are therefore, allowable under Sections 17 VATA.
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 22 of 30

The Appellant asserted that these inputs are evidenced through
valid tax invoices and relevant documentation.
76. The Appellant averred that the Respondent misunderstood its
operations and proceeding to disallow input VAT claimed by the
Appellant on invoices purportedly not declared by a particular
supplier. That the taxpayers from whom the vatable supplies were
obtained were and still are registered for VAT purposes and the
company having carried out its due diligence to confirm the same,
including querying their status from the Respondent’s iTax portal
did not have any reason to disbelief their status.
77. The Tribunal notes that in this regard the Respondent asserted
that the Appellant was granted an opportunity to respond to the
assessments and objected to the assessment after its request to
lodge a late objection was approved. It stated that the Appellant
did not provide any documents in support of its objection and
when reminded to do so it only provided audited accounts. The
Respondent contended that the Appellant failed to provide
documentation required in Section 17 of VATA to enable
determination of deductibility of input VAT. That based on the
above gaps, the Appellant failed to discharge its burden of proof
and therefore, proceeded to confirm the assessments.
78. It was the Respondent’s assertion that the case herein is about
whether transactions amounting to a missing trader scheme were
implemented by the Appellant and aimed at reducing its tax
liability and whether the Appellant successfully rebutted the
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 23 of 30

allegations placed upon it in discharge of its primary burden of
proof. That the Appellant failed to rebut the allegations by
providing positive evidence in support, that it fell short of
discharging its primary burden of proof as provided for under
Section 56 of the TPA and Section 30 of the TATA.
79. In tax matters, the taxpayer has a duty in law to discharging the
burden of proof. Section 56 (1) of the TPA provides that, ‘In any
proceedings under this Part, the burden shall be on the taxpayer
to prove that a tax decision is incorrect.’’
80. Further, Section 30 of the TATA provides as follows:
“In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the appellant has the
burden of proving—
(a) Where an appeal relates to an assessment, that the
assessment is excessive; or
(b) In any other case, that the tax decision should not have
been made or should have been made differently.”
81. The High Court In the case of Commissioner of Domestic Taxes
v Block International Limited [2024] KEHC 8889 (KLR)
stated as follows:
‘‘To his end, I agree with the appellant that pursuant to the
provisions of Section 30 of the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act and
Section 56(1) of the Tax Procedures Act, the respondent
bears the burden of proving that a tax assessment and/or
decision is incorrect.’’
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 24 of 30

82. Further, the High Court in Darwine Wholesalers Limited v
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement (Income
Tax Appeal E051 of 2021) [2023] KEHC 23537 (KLR) held as
follows:
2Under section 59 of the TPA and section 43 of the VAT Act
the Commissioner is expressly empowered to ask for
additional information to ascertain the tax chargeable. This
legal position is in consonance with section 107 and 112 of the
Evidence in that the balance of proof lies with the party with
the knowledge of facts. Further section 30 of the Tax Appeals
Tribunal Act (TATA) and section 56 of the TPA imposes the
burden of proof on the tax payer to prove that an assessment
was wrong or that it was excessive.”
83. To discharge of burden of proof, a taxpayer has a duty to keep
records very well and produce them when called upon to do so,
Section 23 of the TPA is instructive. It provides as follows:
“23. Record-keeping
(1) A person shall—
(a) maintain any document required under a tax law, in
either of the official languages;
(b) maintain any document required under a tax law
so as to enable the person's tax liability to be readily
ascertained; and
(c) subject to subsection (3), retain the document for a period
of five years from the end of the reporting period to which it
relates or such shorter period as may be specified in a tax
law.”
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 25 of 30

84. The assessment in issue related to VAT. In this regard, section 43
of the VATA mandates the taxpayer to keep documents. It
provides as follows:
“43. Keeping of records
(1) A person shall, for the purposes of this Act, keep in the
course of his business, a full and true written record, whether
in electronic form or otherwise, in English or Kiswahili of every
transaction he makes and the record shall be kept for a period
of five years from the date of the last entry made therein.”
85. The Tribunal notes the Appellant’s assertion that it provided
documents and that the input expenses in question were directly
related to the taxable supplies made by the Appellant and were
therefore, allowable under Sections 17 of the VATA. To claim credit
for input tax against output tax, section 17 of the VATA provides
some of the documents that the taxpayer must adduce. In
particular, section 17(3) of the VATA provides as follows:
“(3) The documentation for the purposes of subsection (2)
shall be—
(a) an original tax invoice issued for the supply or a certified
copy;
(b) a customs entry duly certified by the proper officer and a
receipt for the payment of tax;
(c) a customs receipt and a certificate signed by the proper
officer stating the amount of tax paid, in the case of goods
purchased from a customs auction; and
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 26 of 30

(d) a credit note in the case of input tax deducted under
section 16(2);
(e) a debit note in the case of input tax deducted under
section 16(5); or
(f) in the case of a participant in the Open Tender System
for the importation of petroleum products that have been
cleared through a non-bonded facility, the custom entry
showing the name and PIN of the winner of the tender and
the name of the other oil marketing company participating
in the tender:
Provided that the input tax that may have been incurred by
an oil marketing company participating in the Open Tender
System before the coming into force of this provision shall
be claimed within twelve months after this provision comes
into force.”
86. The Tribunal must therefore determine whether the Appellant
adduced documents to justify its claims. The Tribunal perused the
Appellant’s pleadings and noted that the Appellant only attached
its audited accounts for the year 2020. The said audited accounts
in themselves without the primary documents expressly requested
for by the Respondent could in no way demonstrate that input tax
was claimable neither could they demonstrate that the
Respondent erred in confirming the assessment.
87. The Tribunal observed that the Appellant was expressly requested
to provide the following documents:
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 27 of 30

 purchase invoices,
 ETR receipts,
 evidence of delivery/receipt of goods,
 evidence of payments,
 supplier statements; and
 payment schedules.
It failed to provide any of the requested neither did it file a single
document listed under section 17(3) of the VATA.
88. It should be recalled that Section 13(2)(d) of the TATA mandates a
taxpayer to adduced documents to enable the Tribunal make an
informed decision. It provides as follows:
“(2) The appellant shall, within fourteen days from the date
of filing the notice of appeal, submit enough copies, as may
be advised by the Tribunal, of—
(d) such other documents as may be necessary to enable
the Tribunal to make a decision on the appeal.”
89. It is trite law that the Respondent’s assessments enjoy a
presumption of correctness until the same is rebutted by the
taxpayer through production of competent and relevant evidence
as was stated in Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v Trical
and Hard Limited (Tax Appeal E146 of 2020) [2022] KEHC
9927 (KLR) (Commercial and Tax) (8 July 2022) (Judgment)
where the court stated thus:
“A presumption of correctness arises from the Commissioner’s
determination/assessment. The presumption remains until the
taxpayer produces competent and relevant evidence to
support his/her position. When the taxpayer comes forward
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 28 of 30

with such evidence, the presumption vanishes and the case
must be decided upon the evidence presented.”
90. The Tribunal noted that the Appellant failed to file documents that
would have rebutted the presumption of correctness arising out of
the Respondent’s assessments and aided in discharging its burden
of proof.
91. Consequently, the Tribunal finds and holds that the Appellant
failed to discharge its burden of proof under Section 56 of TPA and
Section 30 of TATA.
(b) Whether the Respondent erred in disallowing the
Appellant’s VAT input claims.
92. Having determined that the Appellant failed to discharge its
burden of proof, it follows that the Respondent did not err in
disallowing the Appellants input VAT claims and confirming the
assessed taxes.
FINAL DECISION
93. The upshot to the foregoing is that the Tribunal finds and holds
that the Appeal lacks merit and consequently makes the following
Orders:
(a) The appeal be and is hereby dismissed;
JUDGMENT NO. E1415/2024 BUFFLOC INVESTMENTS LIMITED V COMMISSIONER OF DOMESTIC TAXES
Page 29 of 30

(b) The objection decision dated 7th November, 2024 be and is
hereby upheld; and
(c) Each party to bear its own costs.
94. It is so Ordered.
